era_context: The Late Bronze Age Collapse is approximately 150 years in the future. The eastern Mediterranean and Near East are dominated by the New Kingdom Egypt (19th Dynasty under Ramesses II or his successors), the Hittite Empire at its height, Mycenaean Greece in the Late Helladic IIIB period, and various Levantine city-states. In China, the Shang Dynasty is in its mature phase. This is an era of extensive international trade, diplomatic correspondence (Amarna Letters tradition), and regional great-power competition.

events.title: Reign of Ramesses II continues; possible military campaigns in Nubia or Levant
events.description: Ramesses II's reign (c. 1279–1213 BCE) was marked by extensive military campaigns and monument-building. Around 1670 BCE (within his reign), Egyptian forces were active in Nubia and the Levant, consolidating control and extracting tribute. The exact campaigns of this specific year are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the broader pattern of Egyptian imperial expansion is well-attested.

sources.name: Inscriptions at Abu Simbel and other temples; Ramesside administrative records
Historiographic consensus on Ramesses II's reign chronology

events.certainty_note: Ramesses II's reign is well-dated to c. 1279–1213 BCE, but specific events within individual years are rarely documented unless they were major campaigns or commemorated in inscriptions. This entry reflects the general context rather than a confirmed event for this specific year.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ramesses II's reign itself is subject to ongoing scholarly debate. Some Egyptologists argue for a slightly earlier or later chronology, which would shift all events within his reign by several years. Additionally, the significance of specific military campaigns is often overstated in popular accounts; many were likely routine tribute-collection missions rather than major conquests.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1670 BCE is extremely sparse. We have no contemporary records that specifically document events of this year. Our knowledge comes from: (1) inscriptions and monuments from Ramesses II's reign, which are not year-specific; (2) administrative records that rarely preserve individual-year data; (3) later historiographic synthesis. The surviving record is heavily biased toward Egyptian and Hittite perspectives; events in the Levant, Mycenaean Greece, and elsewhere are known only through fragmentary evidence or later sources. For most regions outside Egypt and the Near East, nothing is known.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses II's reign is part of the broader Late Bronze Age political system that will collapse ~60 years after his death.
